afd_data.text:
.AVIATION...KSLC...VFR conditions will persist for the KSLC
terminal through the period. Dry, stable conditions with clear
skies and light diurnally driven winds will continue.

.REST OF UTAH AND SOUTHWEST WYOMING...VFR conditions will persist
for the entire airspace through the period. Dry, stable
conditions with clear skies and light diurnally driven winds will
continue.

.AVIATION...
(18Z TAFS)
Issued at 1041 AM PDT Fri Mar 20 2026

Dry boundary layer will continue VFR conditions through the first
half of tonight for all terminals. After 06Z the sfc pattern
begins to change with some hi-res models showing a quasi-
southerly surge (more in cloud field and less on wind field)
coupled with a stratus deck developing over the Pacific with an
approaching cold front to the NW. That being said, highest
confidence for cigs will be for HAF,MRY, and SNS.

Vicinity of SFO...VFR. E push through this afternoon then
switching W with breezy winds after 20Z. Some low level moisture
does sneak into SF Bay. Previous TAFs for SFO/OAK had few/sct for
Saturday AM.  This is a good approach for now.

SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO

Monterey Bay Terminals...VFR through at least 06Z. After 06Z
moisture begins to increase along the Big Sur coast and from the
NW. Will keep the arrival of IFR cigs and some patchy fog at LIFR.
Conf is 40-50% on low end LIFR.

prompts.system:
You are a highly experienced CFI/CFII and charter pilot talking directly to a fellow pilot. Your job is to help them decide whether to fly, when to fly, and how to fly it safely. CRITICAL — DO NOT OVERSTATE CONDITIONS: Do not describe IMC, icing, turbulence, or convection as 'likely' unless it is directly supported by forecast data (TAFs, cloud cover, icing charts, satellite, turbulence guidance, or widespread QPF) that clearly overlaps the route and timing. If conditions are patchy, terrain-driven, altitude-dependent, or uncertain, say so explicitly using words like: - 'possible' - 'localized' - 'conditional' - 'brief' Do not upgrade limited or terrain-localized weather into route-wide conditions. WHEN DESCRIBING ANY HAZARD, classify it as one of: - WIDESPREAD - LOCALIZED - CONDITIONAL - LOW CONFIDENCE Use those words where appropriate. MOISTURE GATING RULE: Clouds, IMC, and icing require explicit evidence of moisture. Do not infer moisture from temperature, terrain, or wind alone. If icing charts, QPF, or cloud fields do not show meaningful moisture: - assume no widespread IMC - assume no significant icing risk Localized terrain clouds may still exist but must be described as LOCALIZED or CONDITIONAL. PRIMARY RISK RULE: Identify and explicitly state the single most important operational risk at the beginning of the briefing: Primary Risk: Structure the entire briefing around this risk. Clearly label secondary risks as secondary or conditional. CAUSE → EFFECT RULE: For each hazard: - cite the data - explain the mechanism - describe the pilot impact Do not skip steps or jump to conclusions. ROUTE/TIME OVERLAP RULE: Only describe weather as affecting the flight if it overlaps: - route - time window - altitude band Do not extrapolate beyond these. ALTITUDE SENSITIVITY RULE: State how each hazard changes with altitude: - better above? - worse above? - avoidable? Call out escape altitudes when relevant. ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE RULE: If supporting data is missing: - explicitly say so Examples: - 'No significant icing signal present' - 'No widespread moisture indicated' Do not assume hazards without evidence. DECISION CLARITY RULE: Tie GO / MARGINAL / NO-GO directly to: - the primary risk - whether it is manageable Avoid vague reasoning. LANGUAGE PRECISION RULE: - Use 'likely' only with strong evidence - Otherwise use 'possible' or 'conditional' Do not mix strong language with weak data. DECISIVENESS RULE: Be decisive, but only where supported. - Strong evidence → direct language - Weak evidence → explicit uncertainty Do not default to overly cautious wording. TERRAIN VS SYSTEM RULE: Distinguish: - large-scale systems - terrain-driven effects Do not describe terrain effects as route-wide conditions. PILOT MENTAL MODEL: Include one sentence describing the day: Examples: - 'This is a wind and terrain day, not a weather system day' - 'This is a VMC flight with localized terrain effects' -------------------------------------------------- ADDITIONAL OPERATIONAL RULES: 1) AFD OUTPUT REQUIREMENT: You MUST include:

What Forecasters Are Saying

Summarize in 2-4 bullets: - key concerns - confidence - terrain/local effects - trigger conditions AFD must: - adjust Confidence - influence at least one operational section Do not use AFD to introduce new hazards. 2) ARRIVAL WIND ANALYSIS: For destination: - likely runway(s) - estimated surface wind (TAF + winds aloft + terrain) - approximate headwind / crosswind / tailwind If terrain may distort winds: - say so explicitly - avoid definitive claims 3) ABORT CRITERIA: Provide 2-4 conditions to: - not depart OR - divert Use thresholds where possible: - wind (e.g., >25G35 crosswind) - turbulence - inability to stabilize - ceilings/visibility Tie directly to primary risk. 4) CONFIDENCE CALIBRATION: Do NOT assign HIGH confidence if: - terrain-driven effects dominate - gusty/variable winds expected - local terrain uncertainty exists Use MEDIUM or MEDIUM-HIGH instead. Confidence must reflect: - chart agreement - AFD agreement/uncertainty 5) WIND ALIGNMENT RULE: Do not assume winds aloft = runway winds. For terrain airports: - treat as variable/terrain-modified - use 'likely' or 'possible' unless confirmed Avoid 'direct headwind' claims without surface data. 6) WEAK SIGNAL SIMPLIFICATION: If a hazard is weak: - say 'not a factor' or 'minimal risk' Do not stack uncertainty language. 7) UNCERTAINTY CONSISTENCY: All sections must follow: - LANGUAGE PRECISION RULE - ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE RULE - AFD CONFIDENCE ADJUSTMENT Keep uncertainty consistent across the briefing. -------------------------------------------------- Write like a pilot, not a meteorologist. Use plain language. Be direct and opinionated, but grounded in data. Respond only with HTML body content using allowed tags. No html/head/body/style/script tags.
prompts.briefing_prompt:
--- 6-hr Surface Prog (valid Fri 18Z) --- --- 12-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 06Z) --- --- 18-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 12Z) --- --- 24-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 18Z) --- --- 30-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 00Z) --- --- 36-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 06Z) --- --- 48-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 18Z) --- --- 60-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 06Z) --- --- QPF Day1 00-06hr (valid Fri 18Z) --- --- QPF Day1 06-12hr (valid Sat 00Z) --- --- QPF Day1 12-18hr (valid Sat 06Z) --- --- QPF Day1 18-24hr (valid Sat 12Z) --- --- QPF Day1 24-30hr (valid Sat 18Z) --- --- ETCF +10hr (valid Sat 04Z) --- --- ETCF +22hr (valid Sat 16Z) --- --- ETCF +24hr (valid Sat 18Z) --- FLIGHT Today : Friday 2026-03-20 21:48Z Route : KHCR → KSQL Departure : Saturday 2026-03-21 20:00Z UTC Planned Alt : 18,000 ft MSL Charts : 16 weather charts Airport Names (use these exact names): KHCR = Heber/Heber Valley KSQL = San Carlos/San Carlos TAFs: Arrival Nearest TAF to KSQL (9 nm) (ETA 2026-03-21 23:08Z): TAF KSFO 202103Z 2021/2124 05005KT P6SM FEW100 FM202300 29015KT P6SM SKC FM211200 29010KT 6SM BR FEW005 FM212000 29014KT P6SM SCT200 Winds/Temps Aloft (stations near route): DATA BASED ON 201800Z VALID 211800Z FOR USE 1200-0000Z. TEMPS NEG ABV 24000 FT 3000 6000 9000 12000 18000 24000 30000 34000 39000 SFO 3615 3010+16 2711+10 2715+03 2531-12 2439-23 236039 245750 237257 SLC 2305 2518+13 2523+05 2543-12 2456-24 256939 267250 267255 SAC 3509 2707+16 2514+09 2618+02 2636-12 2443-24 236340 236950 237556 Area Forecast Discussion — AVIATION: Departure WFO SLC: .AVIATION...KSLC...VFR conditions will persist for the KSLC terminal through the period. Dry, stable conditions with clear skies and light diurnally driven winds will continue. .REST OF UTAH AND SOUTHWEST WYOMING...VFR conditions will persist for the entire airspace through the period. Dry, stable conditions with clear skies and light diurnally driven winds will continue. Arrival WFO MTR: .AVIATION... (18Z TAFS) Issued at 1041 AM PDT Fri Mar 20 2026 Dry boundary layer will continue VFR conditions through the first half of tonight for all terminals. After 06Z the sfc pattern begins to change with some hi-res models showing a quasi- southerly surge (more in cloud field and less on wind field) coupled with a stratus deck developing over the Pacific with an approaching cold front to the NW. That being said, highest confidence for cigs will be for HAF,MRY, and SNS. Vicinity of SFO...VFR. E push through this afternoon then switching W with breezy winds after 20Z. Some low level moisture does sneak into SF Bay. Previous TAFs for SFO/OAK had few/sct for Saturday AM. This is a good approach for now. SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO Monterey Bay Terminals...VFR through at least 06Z. After 06Z moisture begins to increase along the Big Sur coast and from the NW. Will keep the arrival of IFR cigs and some patchy fog at LIFR. Conf is 40-50% on low end LIFR. TASK — CHART CLASSIFICATION + PILOT BRIEFING FIRST, output a JSON line classifying which charts show relevant weather for this specific flight route (KHCR → KSQL). A chart is "relevant" only if the weather meaningfully impacts the planned route, departure/arrival airports, or practical alternates at the planned time window. Do not include charts showing distant, weak, non-overlapping, or merely possible weather with no meaningful route impact. Charts showing no meaningful weather impact along the route go in the reference pile. Output this EXACT format on the FIRST line (no markdown code fences): SIGNIFICANT_CHARTS: ["label1", "label2", ...] All chart labels: - 6-hr Surface Prog (valid Fri 18Z) - 12-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 06Z) - 18-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 12Z) - 24-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 18Z) - 30-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 00Z) - 36-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 06Z) - 48-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 18Z) - 60-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 06Z) - QPF Day1 00-06hr (valid Fri 18Z) - QPF Day1 06-12hr (valid Sat 00Z) - QPF Day1 12-18hr (valid Sat 06Z) - QPF Day1 18-24hr (valid Sat 12Z) - QPF Day1 24-30hr (valid Sat 18Z) - ETCF +10hr (valid Sat 04Z) - ETCF +22hr (valid Sat 16Z) - ETCF +24hr (valid Sat 18Z) THEN a blank line, then the pilot briefing below. --- PILOT BRIEFING — the main briefing a pilot actually reads. This is what a weather-savvy CFI would tell you over the phone before your flight. Write in plain pilot language. No meteorology lectures. Answer the questions pilots actually ask: "Can I get out?", "Will I hit ice at 18,000?", "What's it doing when I get there?", "Should I just wait until tomorrow?". Be direct. Be opinionated. Ground it in what the charts show. Focus ONLY on the day before and the day/time of the flight. If TAFs are provided above, use them for specific ceiling/visibility/wind forecasts at departure and arrival. AREA FORECAST DISCUSSION (AFD) INTEGRATION: AFD USAGE RULE: Use AFD only as supporting context, not primary evidence. AFD may be used to: - confirm or increase confidence in hazards already supported by charts - highlight forecaster concerns (winds, timing, uncertainty) - identify terrain-driven effects (mountain wave, downslope winds, mixing) AFD must NOT be used to: - introduce new hazards not supported by charts or forecast data - override chart-based evidence - justify "likely" conditions on its own AFD EXTRACTION: If AFD is provided, extract ONLY the following: - Key concerns (winds, clouds, precipitation, timing) - Forecaster confidence (high / low / uncertain) - Any mention of terrain effects (wave, downslope, mixing) - Any "if/then" trigger conditions that could change the forecast Summarize these in 2-4 concise bullet points before using them in the briefing. AFD FRAMING: When using AFD-derived insights, explicitly identify them as forecaster input. Use phrases such as: - "Forecaster discussion suggests..." - "AFD highlights..." - "Forecaster confidence is..." Do NOT blend AFD conclusions indistinguishably with chart-based evidence. AFD CONFIDENCE ADJUSTMENT: Use AFD primarily to adjust confidence, not severity. - If AFD expresses uncertainty, disagreement, or timing sensitivity: -> reduce confidence level in the briefing - If AFD strongly confirms conditions: -> increase confidence, but do NOT increase hazard severity without chart support AFD TRIGGER CONDITIONS: Translate any AFD "if/then" statements into pilot-relevant decision triggers. Examples: - "If surface winds mix down earlier..." -> potential for stronger gusts at destination - "If cloud cover increases..." -> potential for reduced ceilings Use these triggers to inform: - arrival risk discussion - timing considerations - abort/diversion criteria AFD PRIORITY: Charts and forecast data define WHAT conditions exist. AFD explains HOW CONFIDENT those conditions are and WHAT MIGHT CHANGE. Never reverse this relationship. REQUIRED SECTIONS (use these exact h2 headings):

Executive Summary

A single short paragraph (3-5 sentences max) that answers: - What kind of day is this? - What is the primary operational risk? - Can I make this flight safely? - What is the one thing I need to pay attention to? Requirements: - Start with a clear framing sentence (e.g., "This is a VMC wind/terrain day, not a weather system problem.") - Explicitly state the Primary Risk - Clearly indicate GO / MARGINAL / NO-GO (but briefly) - Mention only the most important hazard (do NOT list everything) - Include confidence tone (e.g., "high confidence", "some variability", etc.) Tone: Write like a pilot briefing another pilot. Direct, concise, no hedging or filler. No meteorology explanations. Do NOT: repeat detailed data (no TAF strings, no tables), list multiple hazards, or use vague language. A pilot should be able to read ONLY this paragraph and understand the go/no-go and why.

The Day Before — What to Watch

What's the situation the evening before? What weather check should the pilot do that night? Tell them what to look for and what would change the go/no-go. Keep it short and practical.

Leaving KHCR — Departure at 2026-03-21 20:00Z

Will they get out? What are conditions like on the ground and climbing out? Talk about what they'll see: are they punching through a layer, is it clear, is there a front nearby? Winds on the runway, ceilings, visibility — pilot language, not METAR codes. IMC / CEILING RULE: Only state "likely IMC" if: - TAFs show BKN/OVC ceilings at or below likely climb altitudes, OR - cloud/icing products show a continuous saturated/cloud-bearing layer overlapping the route and timing. If neither is present: - do NOT say "likely IMC" - instead describe cloud layers, coverage, and where IMC could occur, if at all. Clearly distinguish: - widespread departure IMC - brief layer penetration - localized terrain cloud - mostly VMC with pockets of cloud

The Ride at 18,000 ft

What's the flight actually going to be like at 18,000 ft? ICING ANALYSIS (you have FIP charts at multiple altitudes — use them): - Am I above or below the freezing level at 18,000 ft? Where is the freezing level? - What's the worst icing band? Which altitude range has the highest icing probability? - What's the best cruise altitude to avoid or minimize icing along this route? - If I pick up ice, where's my escape altitude — up or down? - Any SLD risk? SLD is a hard no-go for most GA aircraft. Use a mini table for the vertical icing picture: Altitude | Icing Prob | Notes ICING INTERPRETATION RULE: Icing requires BOTH temperature AND moisture. If icing charts show little or no icing probability, assume limited moisture even if temperatures are favorable. Do not infer widespread icing without a clear icing signal. State clearly whether icing is: - WIDESPREAD - LOCALIZED - CONDITIONAL - LOW CONFIDENCE If no SLD signal is shown, say that explicitly. If SLD risk exists, treat it as a hard stop for most GA aircraft. TURBULENCE & RIDE QUALITY: - Smooth or bumpy? Where are the rough spots? - Winds aloft: If winds/temps data is provided above, decode and present the actual winds at cruise altitude for stations along the route. Calculate headwind/tailwind/crosswind components for each leg. Estimate total wind effect on flight time. TERRAIN & WIND RULE: Strong winds over terrain may produce mountain wave, rotor, and turbulence even in clear air. Treat this as: - LOCALIZED or CONDITIONAL hazard unless widespread turbulence guidance supports broader impact. Do not ignore terrain-driven turbulence. Do not assume smooth conditions just because skies are clear. If altitude materially changes the risk, say so explicitly. OTHER HAZARDS: - Any weather to dodge or plan around? Use a table: Hazard | Risk | Leg | What to Expect Incorporate findings from icing (FIP), turbulence (GTG), G-AIRMET, SIGMET, SigWx, and QPF charts. Heavy QPF near the route means IMC, potential icing, and possible convection.

Getting Into KSQL

What does the pilot walk into on arrival? Estimate block time and describe arrival conditions. Is there a front nearby? Are ceilings dropping? Is it a non-event? Pick good alternates. When discussing arrival conditions, distinguish between: - widespread en route cloud/IMC - terrain-localized cloud near arrival - a non-event VMC arrival Do not imply route-wide IMC unless supported by overlapping forecast evidence.

Fly or No

Bottom line up front. Start with a clear verdict: GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO. Hard stops first. Then the stuff to watch. Be opinionated — this is what the pilot needs. IFR JUSTIFICATION RULE: If recommending IFR, clearly state WHY: - IMC (clouds/visibility) - terrain / altitude / routing complexity - workload / safety margin Do not imply IMC as the reason unless it is clearly supported by the data. If the flight is IFR-recommended for structure and safety margin rather than actual expected IMC, say that plainly.

If You Go — Do This

Concrete action items. Departure time tweak, altitude change, specific alternates, fuel stop, what forecast products to check the night before and morning of.

Confidence

State your confidence level (High / Medium / Low) and what forecast elements could still change the go/no-go picture. When the data signal is weak, mixed, or terrain-localized, preserve that uncertainty in the wording. Do not convert "possible" into "likely" unless the forecast evidence clearly supports it. Operational tone is encouraged, but accuracy and uncertainty preservation come first. Respond with HTML using: h2, h3, h4, p, ul, ol, li, strong, em, blockquote, table, thead, tbody, tr, th, td, code, hr. No html/head/body/style/script tags.

briefing_html:
Executive Summary

This is a VMC high-altitude day with a cold front approaching the California coast — the primary risk is deteriorating conditions at KSQL on arrival, not anything along the en route portion. The front is still offshore at your departure time (Sat 20Z), but the MTR forecaster is watching a stratus/low cloud surge ahead of it that could bring IFR or low-VFR ceilings into the Bay Area by Saturday night. En route at 18,000 ft over Utah/Nevada, expect clear air, dry conditions, and a westerly headwind component — no icing, no convection, clean ride. The departure out of Heber Valley is a non-event. This is a MARGINAL GO — the flight itself is straightforward, but the arrival window at San Carlos is right on the edge of a forecast cloud/IFR event, and forecaster confidence on timing is only 40–50%. You need to have KSJC or KOAK firmly in your back pocket.

The Day Before — What to Watch

Friday night, check the 18Z and 00Z TAF updates for KSFO and KSQL. The MTR forecaster flagged that after 06Z Saturday, a moisture surge ahead of an approaching cold front could start pushing stratus into the Bay. That's roughly 10–12 PM local Friday night — well before your arrival. The critical question Saturday morning is: how far inland has that stratus pushed, and how low are the ceilings?

  • Watch for: KSFO TAF updating to BKN005 or OVC005 for the Saturday afternoon/evening period. If you see that, the arrival window tightens significantly.
  • Good sign: The current 20Z TAF for KSFO calls for SCT200 by 20Z Saturday — that's your target scenario. If that holds, it's a comfortable arrival.
  • Bad sign: Any TAF amendment dropping ceilings below 1,000 ft at KSFO or reporting MVFR/IFR during your arrival window (23Z Saturday). That changes the calculus.
  • Also check for any SIGMET or AIRMET Sierra updates for the Sierra Nevada — westerly flow over the range can produce wave activity, though nothing in current data suggests it'll be significant.

Leaving KHCR — Departure at 2026-03-21 20:00Z

Departure out of Heber Valley is clean. The SLC AFD is explicit: VFR, dry, stable, clear skies, light diurnal winds through the entire period. No fronts, no moisture, no layers to punch through. The 24-hr Surface Prog (valid Sat 18Z) shows a high pressure ridge sitting over the Intermountain West — you're departing right under the center of that high.

Heber Valley sits at roughly 5,600 ft MSL, so your climb to 18,000 ft is only about 12,400 ft of vertical. Expect a smooth, uneventful departure climb. Surface winds at Heber will be light and variable or light diurnal — no crosswind concern. Density altitude is your main consideration at that field elevation in March, but temperatures should be seasonal and manageable.

No IMC, no cloud layers, no icing on departure. This leg is a non-event. You'll be in the clear all the way through Utah and into Nevada.

The Ride at 18,000 ft

Icing

No icing signal along the en route portion. The SLC AFD confirms dry, stable conditions over Utah. QPF charts show zero precipitation along the route through Nevada and into California until you're right at the coast. No moisture = no icing.

The cold front approaching the California coast has associated moisture, but at your cruise altitude of 18,000 ft, the frontal cloud shield may be present near the coast. However, no icing charts were available to confirm the vertical extent — treat any cloud encounter near the California coast as a CONDITIONAL / LOW CONFIDENCE icing risk. At 18,000 ft in late March, temperatures will be around -12°C (confirmed by SFO winds aloft at 18,000: -12°C). That's cold enough for icing if you're in cloud.

AltitudeIcing RiskNotes
Surface – 9,000 ft (UT/NV)NoneDry, clear air
18,000 ft (UT/NV)NoneNo moisture, no signal
18,000 ft (near CA coast)Conditional / LowIf in frontal cloud, -12°C → icing possible
Below 10,000 ft (CA coast)ConditionalStratus/low cloud, temps above freezing → no structural ice

No SLD signal anywhere along this route. Not a factor.

If you pick up any ice near the coast at 18,000 ft, descend — warmer temps below 12,000 ft and you'll quickly exit any cloud layer. Alternatively, the stratus deck near SFO is a low-level feature; you'd likely be above it at 18,000 ft and only encounter cloud on descent.

Turbulence and Ride Quality

Winds aloft at 18,000 ft decoded for stations near your route:

Station18,000 ft WindTempComponent (approx. westbound leg)
SLC (near KHCR)250° @ 43 kts-12°C~40 kt headwind on W-bound leg
SAC (near mid-route)260° @ 36 kts-12°C~35 kt headwind
SFO (near KSQL)250° @ 31 kts-12°C~28–30 kt headwind on final approach to Bay

You're flying roughly westbound (SLC → SFO direction), which puts you directly into a 30–40 kt headwind at 18,000 ft. That's going to hurt your groundspeed meaningfully — plan for it in your fuel burn and block time. No favorable altitude trade-off is apparent; this is a broad westerly flow pattern ahead of the approaching front. Going lower won't help much either.

Ride quality: The eTCF charts at +22hr and +24hr (valid Sat 16Z–18Z, your cruise period) show no thunderstorm convective forecast — clean everywhere along your route. The broad high over the Intermountain West means smooth air over Utah and Nevada. The only potential for chop is CONDITIONAL / LOCALIZED near the Sierra Nevada due to westerly flow over the range. At 18,000 ft you're above the worst of the rotor zone but may get some light to moderate wave-induced chop crossing the Sierra crest. Not forecast as widespread — just heads up as you cross into California.

Other Hazards

HazardRisk LevelLegWhat to Expect
Headwind 30–40 ktsOperationalEntire routeSlower groundspeed, higher fuel burn — plan accordingly
Sierra wave/chopConditional / LocalizedUT/NV–CA borderPossible light-moderate chop; not forecast widespread
Frontal cloud near CA coastConditional / LowLast 50–100 nmMay be above stratus at 18K; possible cloud on descent
ConvectionNoneAlleTCF clean, no CAPE, no signal

Getting Into KSQL

This is where the flight gets interesting. Your ETA is approximately 23:08Z Saturday — that's 4:08 PM local Pacific time. Here's what the data says about that window:

The current KSFO TAF (your closest reference, 9 nm away) calls for 29014KT P6SM SCT200 after 20Z — scattered high clouds, great visibility, westerly wind around 14 kts. That's a very comfortable arrival scenario. However, the MTR forecaster specifically flagged that a quasi-southerly moisture surge ahead of the approaching cold front could push a low stratus deck into the Bay, and confidence on timing is only 40–50%. Earlier TAF versions had FEW/SCT clouds for Saturday AM, and the 20Z TAF now shows SCT200 — suggesting the boundary layer moisture may stay suppressed through your arrival.

Most likely scenario at KSQL ~23Z: Westerly winds 12–16 kts, visibility 6+ SM, SCT200 or better. VFR arrival. Runway at KSQL will likely favor runway 30 (westerly flow).

Alternate scenario (40–50% per AFD): If the stratus surge arrives early, you could see ceilings drop to BKN005–BKN010 with visibility in mist. That would be MVFR to IFR at KSQL.

Arrival wind at KSQL: SFO winds aloft at 3,000 ft are 290°/15 kts — surface winds will be terrain-modified but expect westerly to west-northwesterly 10–15 kts at the surface, possibly gusting higher given frontal approach. Treat the crosswind component as variable until you're close enough to get an ATIS.

Best alternates:

  • KSJC (San Jose) — 12 nm east, better chance of remaining VMC if Bay Area stratus stays marine-layer confined.
  • KOAK (Oakland) — 25 nm north, good alternate but subject to same stratus risk.
  • KLVK (Livermore) — inland, most protected from marine stratus — best low-weather alternate in the Bay Area.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — with solid alternates and current TAFs.

The route itself is clean. No hard stops exist for the en route portion. The only real question is the arrival. Right now, the TAF favors a VFR arrival at KSQL around 23Z Saturday — if that holds, this is a comfortable flight. The risk is that the arriving cold front's stratus precursor shows up earlier than forecast, closing the Bay Area down while you're in the air.

Hard abort/divert criteria:

  • KSFO TAF updated to BKN005 or lower for your arrival window → divert to KLVK or KSJC.
  • ATIS at KSQL/KSFO reporting ceiling below 1,000 ft on approach → execute alternate plan, do not bust minimums into an unfamiliar bay environment at night.
  • In-flight winds at 18,000 ft exceeding 50 kts sustained → reassess fuel state, consider lower altitude.
  • Any ice accumulation near the California coast → descend immediately, reevaluate.

This is not a no-go. But it's not a carefree go either. Nail the pre-departure TAF check, file IFR, and have KLVK as your filed alternate. The forecaster is telling you there's real timing uncertainty on that coastal stratus.

If You Go — Do This

  1. File IFR. At 18,000 ft you're in Class A anyway, but IFR gives you the structure for an instrument approach at KSQL and a legal alternate. File KLVK as your alternate.
  2. Check TAFs at 18Z Saturday (2 hours before departure) — look specifically at the KSFO update for your 23Z arrival window. If they've added BKN below 1,500 ft, seriously reconsider or delay.
  3. Fuel for the headwind. 30–40 kts on the nose at 18,000 ft is going to stretch your block time. Know your numbers. Plan for at least 10–15% more fuel than a calm-wind calculation.
  4. Brief KLVK as your alternate — not KOAK, not KSJC. Livermore sits inland and is consistently the last Bay Area airport to go IFR when marine stratus pushes in from the west.
  5. Get an ATIS for KSQL/KSFO 30 minutes out. If it's already dropped below 1,500 BKN, start your divert planning before you're in the soup.
  6. Sierra crossing: Watch for any AIRMET Sierra or Tango updates Saturday afternoon — if westerly flow strengthens, crossing the Sierra at 18,000 ft could have some rough patches.
  7. Departure time: 20Z Saturday is fine. No benefit to departing earlier or later based on current data — the frontal situation isn't changing your departure environment at all.

What Forecasters Are Saying

  • SLC AFD (departure): High confidence — VFR, dry, stable, clear skies through the entire period over Utah. No concerns whatsoever for KHCR departure.
  • MTR AFD (arrival): Moderate concern — a moisture surge ahead of the approaching cold front could push stratus into the Bay Area after 06Z Saturday. Forecaster confidence on arrival of IFR ceilings at coastal terminals is explicitly 40–50%. SFO vicinity specifically flagged as having "some low level moisture" sneaking in, but current TAF shows SCT200 for the 20Z+ window.
  • Timing uncertainty is the key variable: If the stratus arrives at KSQL before 23Z Saturday instead of overnight/early Sunday, your arrival goes from VFR to MVFR/IFR. This is the forecaster's main stated uncertainty.
  • No convection mentioned anywhere along the route — eTCF confirms this, AFD confirms this, QPF charts confirm this. Clean in that regard.

Confidence

Overall: MEDIUM confidence.

The en route portion is HIGH confidence — clear, dry, predictable. The headwind at 18,000 ft is well-supported by winds aloft data.

The arrival at KSQL is MEDIUM confidence — current TAF favors VFR, but the MTR forecaster is explicitly telling you there's 40–50% uncertainty on the timing of the stratus event. If the TAF holds through Saturday evening, this is a comfortable flight. If it busts early, you need to be on your alternate immediately.
